mn54:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Aṅguttarāpans, at a market town of the Aṅguttarāpans named Āpaṇa.
mn54:10.1: “‘By relying on non-covetousness, covetousness is to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:10.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:10.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might be covetous.
mn54:10.4: And if I were covetous, I would reproach myself because of covetousness, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of covetousness, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of covetousness.
mn54:10.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, covetousness.
mn54:10.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of covetousness do not exist for one who has abstained from covetousness in this way.’
mn54:10.7: ‘By relying on non-covetousness, covetousness is to be abandoned’—
mn54:10.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:11.1: “‘By relying on non-denigration and non-resentment, denigration and resentment are to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:11.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:11.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might be denigrating and resentful.
mn54:11.4: And if I were denigrating and resentful, I would reproach myself because of denigration and resentment, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of denigration and resentment, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of denigration and resentment.
mn54:11.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, denigration and resentment.
mn54:11.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of denigration and resentment do not exist for one who is free from denigration and resentment in this way.’
mn54:11.7: ‘By relying on non-denigration and non-resentment, denigration and resentment are to be abandoned’—
mn54:11.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:12.1: “‘By relying on non-anger and non-distress, anger and distress are to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:12.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:12.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might be angry and distressed.
mn54:12.4: And if I were angry and distressed, I would reproach myself because of anger and distress, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of anger and distress, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of anger and distress.
mn54:12.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, anger and distress.
mn54:12.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of anger and distress do not exist for one who is free from anger and distress in this way.’
mn54:12.7: ‘By relying on non-anger and non-distress, anger and distress are to be abandoned’—
mn54:12.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:13.1: “‘By relying on non-arrogance, arrogance is to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:13.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:13.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might be arrogant.
mn54:13.4: And if I were arrogant, I would reproach myself because of arrogance, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of arrogance, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of arrogance.
mn54:13.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, arrogance.
mn54:13.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of arrogance do not exist for one who is free from arrogance in this way.’
mn54:13.7: ‘By relying on non-arrogance, arrogance is to be abandoned’—
mn54:13.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:14.1: These eight *dhammas*, householder, have been stated in brief and explained in detail; they lead to the cutting off of dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*.
mn54:14.2: But even so, that is not yet in every respect the complete cutting off of all dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*.”
mn54:14.3: “But in what way, *bhante*, is there in every respect the complete cutting off of all dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*?
mn54:14.4: It would be good if the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, would teach me the *dhamma* in such a way that in every respect there is the complete cutting off of all dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*.”
mn54:14.5: “Then listen, householder, and attend carefully. I will speak.”
mn54:14.6: “Yes, *bhante*,” Potaliya the householder replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn54:15.0: 1. On the Drawbacks of Sensual Pleasures
mn54:15.1: “Suppose, householder, a dog, overcome by hunger and weakness, were standing by a butcher’s block.
mn54:15.10: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a skeleton—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’
mn54:15.11: Having seen this as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], having turned away from this equanimity [*upekkhā*] that is diverse, dependent on diversity, he develops only that equanimity [*upekkhā*] that is unitary, dependent on unity, where clingings/feedings on worldly bait [*lokāmisūpādānā*] all cease without remainder.
mn54:15.2: Then a skilled butcher or a butcher’s apprentice were to toss him a skeleton, well scraped, cleanly stripped of meat, smeared with blood.
mn54:15.4: would that dog, by licking that skeleton, well scraped, cleanly stripped of meat, smeared with blood, get rid of hunger and weakness?”
mn54:15.7: “Because, *bhante*, that skeleton is well scraped, cleanly stripped of meat, smeared with blood.
mn54:15.8: That dog would only come to a share in weariness and frustration because of it.”
mn54:15.9: “In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:16.1: “Suppose, householder, a vulture or a hawk or a falcon were to seize a lump of meat and fly off with it.
mn54:16.2: Then vultures and hawks and falcons would swoop down on it again and again, and tear at it and pull at it.
mn54:16.4: if that vulture or hawk or falcon did not quickly let go of that lump of meat, would it come to death or deadly *dukkha* because of that?”
mn54:16.6: “In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:16.7: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a lump of meat—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’
mn54:16.8: Having seen this as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], having turned away from this equanimity [*upekkhā*] that is diverse, dependent on diversity, he develops only that equanimity [*upekkhā*] that is unitary, dependent on unity, where clingings/feedings on worldly bait [*lokāmisūpādānā*] all cease without remainder.
mn54:17.1: “Suppose, householder, a man were to go against the wind carrying a burning grass torch.
mn54:17.3: if that man did not quickly let go of that burning grass torch, would that burning grass torch burn his hand or burn his arm or burn some other part or another of his body, and would he come to death or deadly *dukkha* because of that?”
mn54:17.5: “In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:17.6: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a grass torch—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’
mn54:17.7: Having seen this as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] … he develops only that equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn54:18.1: “Suppose, householder, there were a pit of glowing coals, deeper than a man’s height, full of coals without flame and without smoke.
mn54:18.10: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a pit of coals—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’
mn54:18.11: Having seen this as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] … he develops only that equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn54:18.2: Then a man might come along, wanting to live, not wanting to die, wanting *sukha*, averse to *dukkha*.
mn54:18.3: And two strong men, grasping him by both arms, might drag him toward that pit of coals.
mn54:18.5: would that man twist his body this way and that?”
mn54:18.8: “Because, *bhante*, it is known to that man: ‘If I fall into this pit of coals, because of that I will come to death or deadly *dukkha*.’”
mn54:18.9: “In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:19.1: “Suppose, householder, a man were to see a dream: a delightful park, a delightful forest, delightful ground, delightful lotus ponds.
mn54:19.2: On waking, he would not see anything at all.
mn54:19.3: In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:19.4: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a dream—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’ …
mn54:2.1: Then, in the morning, the *Bhagavā* robed himself, took his bowl and robe, and entered Āpaṇa for alms.
mn54:2.2: After walking for alms in Āpaṇa, when he had returned from his almsround, after the meal, he went to a certain woodland for the day’s abiding.
mn54:2.3: Having entered that woodland, he sat down at the root of a certain tree for the day’s abiding.
mn54:20.1: “Suppose, householder, a man were to borrow goods—a vehicle, attendants, and fine jewel earrings.
mn54:20.10: “In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:20.11: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like borrowed goods—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’ …
mn54:20.2: Escorted and surrounded by those borrowed goods, he might proceed through the marketplace.
mn54:20.4: ‘This man is surely wealthy; this is how wealthy people enjoy their wealth!’
mn54:20.5: But the owners, wherever they saw him, would take those things away right there.
mn54:20.6: What do you think, householder, would that be enough to upset that man?”
mn54:20.9: “Because, *bhante*, the owners are taking them away.”
mn54:21.1: “Suppose, householder, not far from a village or market town there were a dense woodland.
mn54:21.10: Then a second man might come along wanting fruit, seeking fruit, wandering in search of fruit, carrying a sharp axe.
mn54:21.11: Having entered that woodland, he would see that tree, laden with fruit and bearing fruit.
mn54:21.12: The thought would occur to him:
mn54:21.13: ‘This tree is laden with fruit and bearing fruit, and no fruits have fallen to the ground.
mn54:21.14: But I do not know how to climb a tree.
mn54:21.15: What if I cut this tree down at the root, eat as much as I want, and fill my lap?’
mn54:21.16: Then he would cut that tree down at the root.
mn54:21.18: that first man who had climbed the tree—if he did not quickly come down, when that tree fell would it break his hand or break his foot or break some other part or another of his body, and would he come to death or deadly *dukkha* because of that?”
mn54:21.2: And there was a tree there, laden with fruit and bearing fruit, but no fruits had fallen to the ground.
mn54:21.20: “In the same way, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:21.21: ‘Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like the fruit of a tree—having much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’
mn54:21.22: Having seen this as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*], having turned away from this equanimity [*upekkhā*] that is diverse, dependent on diversity, he develops only that equanimity [*upekkhā*] that is unitary, dependent on unity, where clingings/feedings on worldly bait [*lokāmisūpādānā*] all cease without remainder.
mn54:21.3: Then a man might come along wanting fruit, seeking fruit, wandering in search of fruit.
mn54:21.4: Having entered that woodland, he would see that tree, laden with fruit and bearing fruit.
mn54:21.5: The thought would occur to him:
mn54:21.6: ‘This tree is laden with fruit and bearing fruit, and no fruits have fallen to the ground.
mn54:21.7: Now I know how to climb a tree.
mn54:21.8: What if I climb this tree, eat as much as I want, and fill my lap?’
mn54:21.9: Then he would climb that tree, eat as much as he wanted, and fill his lap.
mn54:22.1: “Then, householder, that noble disciple, by attaining this very unsurpassed purification of *sati* by equanimity [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], recollects his many kinds of past lives,
mn54:22.2: that is: one birth, two births … Thus with features and details he recollects his many kinds of past lives.
mn54:23.1: Then, householder, that noble disciple, by attaining this very unsurpassed purification of *sati* by equanimity [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, sees beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in good destinations and bad destinations … He understands beings as faring on according to their *kamma*.
mn54:24.1: Then, householder, that noble disciple, by attaining this very unsurpassed purification of *sati* by equanimity [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*], with the destruction of the *āsavas*, dwells having realized for himself with direct knowledge, in this very life, the liberation of mind free from *āsavas* [*anāsavaṁ cetovimuttiṁ*] and liberation by discernment [*paññāvimuttiṁ*], having attained it.
mn54:25.1: To this extent, householder, there is in every respect the complete cutting off of all dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*.
mn54:25.10: Indeed, *bhante*, the *Bhagavā* has aroused in me love for ascetics, confidence in ascetics, and reverence for ascetics.
mn54:25.3: when there is in every respect the complete cutting off of all dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*, do you see such a cutting off of dealings in yourself?”
mn54:25.4: “What am I, *bhante*, compared with the complete cutting off of all dealings in every respect in the noble one’s *vinaya*?
mn54:25.5: I am far away, *bhante*, from the complete cutting off of all dealings in every respect in the noble one’s *vinaya*.
mn54:25.6: For formerly, *bhante*, we regarded wandering ascetics of other sects, though they were not thoroughbreds, as thoroughbreds; though they were not thoroughbreds, we fed them the food for thoroughbreds; though they were not thoroughbreds, we placed them in the place for thoroughbreds.
mn54:25.7: But *bhikkhus*, *bhante*, though they were thoroughbreds, we regarded as not thoroughbreds; though they were thoroughbreds, we fed them the food for those not thoroughbreds; though they were thoroughbreds, we placed them in the place for those not thoroughbreds.
mn54:25.8: But now, *bhante*, we shall know wandering ascetics of other sects, though they are not thoroughbreds, as not thoroughbreds; though they are not thoroughbreds, we shall feed them the food for those not thoroughbreds; though they are not thoroughbreds, we shall place them in the place for those not thoroughbreds.
mn54:25.9: But *bhikkhus*, *bhante*, though they are thoroughbreds, we shall know as thoroughbreds; though they are thoroughbreds, we shall feed them the food for thoroughbreds; though they are thoroughbreds, we shall place them in the place for thoroughbreds.
mn54:26.2: Just as if, *bhante*, one were to set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what had been concealed, or point out the way to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the darkness, thinking, ‘Those with eyes will see forms’—just so has the *dhamma* been made clear in many ways by the *Bhagavā*.
mn54:26.3: I go for refuge to the *Bhagavā*, to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn54:26.4: May the *Bhagavā* remember me as a lay follower who from today has gone for refuge for life.”
mn54:26.5: The Potaliya Sutta is finished, the fourth.
mn54:3.1: Then Potaliya the householder, well dressed in cloak and tunic, with parasol and sandals, while walking and strolling for exercise, went to that woodland. Having entered that woodland, he approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
mn54:3.10: “But those are your features, householder; those are your marks; those are your *nimittas*, just as for a householder.”
mn54:3.11: “But, Master Gotama, all my undertakings have been rejected, all my dealings cut off.”
mn54:3.12: “But in what way, householder, have all your undertakings been rejected and all your dealings cut off?”
mn54:3.13: “Here, Master Gotama, whatever wealth or grain or silver or gold I had, all that has been handed over to my sons as inheritance. In regard to that, I give no advice and make no reproach; I live concerned only with food and clothing.
mn54:3.14: That is how, Master Gotama, all my undertakings have been rejected and all my dealings cut off.”
mn54:3.15: “Householder, what you call the cutting off of dealings is one thing, but in the noble one’s *vinaya* the cutting off of dealings is something else.”
mn54:3.16: “But in what way, *bhante*, does the cutting off of dealings occur in the noble one’s *vinaya*?
mn54:3.17: It would be good if the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, would teach me the *dhamma* in such a way that the cutting off of dealings occurs in the noble one’s *vinaya*.”
mn54:3.18: “Then listen, householder, and attend carefully. I will speak.”
mn54:3.19: “Yes, *bhante*,” Potaliya the householder replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn54:3.2: When he had concluded courteous and memorable conversation, he stood to one side. While Potaliya the householder was standing to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to him:
mn54:3.3: “There are seats here, householder. If you wish, sit down.”
mn54:3.4: When this was said, Potaliya the householder, thinking, “The ascetic Gotama addresses me as ‘householder,’” became angry and displeased, and remained silent.
mn54:3.6: A third time the *Bhagavā* said this to Potaliya the householder:
mn54:3.7: “There are seats here, householder. If you wish, sit down.”
mn54:3.8: When this was said, Potaliya the householder, thinking, “The ascetic Gotama addresses me as ‘householder,’” angry and displeased, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn54:3.9: “This is not proper, Master Gotama, this is not fitting, that you address me as ‘householder.’”
mn54:4.10: by relying on non-anger and non-distress, anger and distress are to be abandoned;
mn54:4.11: by relying on non-arrogance, arrogance is to be abandoned.
mn54:4.12: These eight *dhammas*, householder, have been stated in brief, not explained in detail; they lead to the cutting off of dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*.”
mn54:4.2: “These eight *dhammas*, householder, lead to the cutting off of dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*.
mn54:4.4: By relying on non-killing, killing is to be abandoned;
mn54:4.5: by relying on taking what is given, taking what is not given is to be abandoned;
mn54:4.6: by relying on truthful speech, false speech is to be abandoned;
mn54:4.7: by relying on non-divisive speech, divisive speech is to be abandoned;
mn54:4.8: by relying on non-covetousness, covetousness is to be abandoned;
mn54:4.9: by relying on non-denigration and non-resentment, denigration and resentment are to be abandoned;
mn54:5.1: “The eight *dhammas* that have been stated in brief by the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, and not explained in detail, which lead to the cutting off of dealings in the noble one’s *vinaya*—it would be good if the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, would explain these eight *dhammas* to me in detail, out of compassion.”
mn54:5.2: “Then listen, householder, and attend carefully. I will speak.”
mn54:5.3: “Yes, *bhante*,” Potaliya the householder replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn54:6.1: “‘By relying on non-killing, killing is to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:6.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:6.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might kill living beings.
mn54:6.4: And if I were one who kills living beings, I would reproach myself because of killing living beings, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of killing living beings, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of killing living beings.
mn54:6.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, killing living beings.
mn54:6.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of killing living beings do not exist for one who has abstained from killing living beings in this way.’
mn54:6.7: ‘By relying on non-killing, killing is to be abandoned’—
mn54:6.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:7.1: “‘By relying on taking what is given, taking what is not given is to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:7.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:7.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might take what is not given.
mn54:7.4: And if I were one who takes what is not given, I would reproach myself because of taking what is not given, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of taking what is not given, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of taking what is not given.
mn54:7.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, taking what is not given.
mn54:7.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of taking what is not given do not exist for one who has abstained from taking what is not given in this way.’
mn54:7.7: ‘By relying on taking what is given, taking what is not given is to be abandoned’—
mn54:7.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:8.1: “‘By relying on truthful speech, false speech is to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:8.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:8.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might speak falsely.
mn54:8.4: And if I were one who speaks falsely, I would reproach myself because of false speech, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of false speech, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of false speech.
mn54:8.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, false speech.
mn54:8.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of false speech do not exist for one who has abstained from false speech in this way.’
mn54:8.7: ‘By relying on truthful speech, false speech is to be abandoned’—
mn54:8.8: this was said in dependence on this.
mn54:9.1: “‘By relying on non-divisive speech, divisive speech is to be abandoned’—this was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn54:9.2: Here, householder, a noble disciple reflects thus:
mn54:9.3: ‘I am practicing for the abandoning and eradication of the fetters because of which I might speak divisively.
mn54:9.4: And if I were one who speaks divisively, I would reproach myself because of divisive speech, the wise would blame me after considering the matter because of divisive speech, and after the breakup of the body, after death, a bad destination could be expected because of divisive speech.
mn54:9.5: Now this itself is the fetter, this is the hindrance—namely, divisive speech.
mn54:9.6: And the *āsavas*, afflictions, and fevers that might arise because of divisive speech do not exist for one who has abstained from divisive speech in this way.’
mn54:9.7: ‘By relying on non-divisive speech, divisive speech is to be abandoned’—
mn54:9.8: this was said in dependence on this.
